periods.objective: Distinguish functions from non-functions using tables, graphs, and mappings.
periods.workedExamples: 8-1, Examples 1-3 — Determine if a Relation Is a Function from a Table; Vertical Line Test on a Graph; Determine if a Mapping Diagram Is a Function
periods.notes: Function definition, vertical line test, and mapping diagrams.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8a with one retrieval prompt defining a function as a relation where each input has exactly one output.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-1, Examples 1-3 to develop: determine if a table represents a function by checking for repeated inputs, apply the vertical line test on a graph, and analyze mapping diagrams for one-to-one or one-to-many relationships.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-1, Examples 1-3: test tables, graphs, and mappings for function status; teacher checks one-output-per-input reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_definition: function identification from tables, graphs, and mapping diagrams.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one function identification from a table and one from a graph.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with distinguishing functions from non-functions.

periods.objective: Find domain and range from various representations.
periods.workedExamples: 8-1, Examples 4-5 — Find the Domain and Range of a Relation; Real-World Input-Output Machine; 8-2, Examples 1-2 — Domain and Range from a Continuous Graph; Domain and Range from a Discrete Graph
periods.notes: Domain and range from ordered pairs, continuous/discrete graphs; real-world input-output.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8b with one retrieval prompt defining domain (all inputs) and range (all outputs).
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-1, Examples 4-5 and 8-2, Examples 1-2 to develop: find domain and range from ordered pairs and relation tables, interpret a real-world input-output machine, and distinguish domain/range from continuous vs. discrete graphs.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on the worked examples: identify domain and range from different representations; teacher checks interval notation and set notation.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on domain_range: domain and range from tables, graphs, and ordered pairs.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one domain-and-range problem from a graph and one from a table.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with finding domain and range.

periods.objective: Determine domain restrictions from algebraic expressions.
periods.workedExamples: 8-2, Examples 3-4 — Domain Restrictions (Square Root and Denominator); Domain and Range from a Mapping
periods.notes: Focus on identifying values that make square roots undefined or denominators zero, and domain/range from mapping diagrams.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8c with one retrieval prompt on what values make a square root undefined or a denominator zero.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-2, Examples 3-4 to develop: identify domain restrictions from square root expressions (radicand must be non-negative) and denominator expressions (denominator cannot be zero), and determine domain and range from a mapping diagram.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-2, Examples 3-4: find restricted domains from algebraic expressions; teacher checks restriction identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on domain_range: domain restriction problems from square roots and denominators.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one square-root domain restriction and one denominator domain restriction problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with domain restrictions.

periods.objective: Apply domain and range to real-world contexts; evaluate functions using function notation.
periods.workedExamples: 8-2, Example 5 — Real-World Projectile Domain and Range; 8-3, Examples 1-2 — Evaluate f(3) for f(x) = 2x² − 1; Find x When f(x) = 7
periods.notes: Contextual domain/range from projectile motion and introduction to function evaluation. Split from Period 3 to separate algebraic restriction identification from function-evaluation practice.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8c with one retrieval prompt on how domain and range apply to a projectile's height over time.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-2, Example 5 and 8-3, Examples 1-2 to develop: determine domain and range from a real-world projectile context (time and height constraints), evaluate functions at specific inputs using f(x) notation, and solve for x given a function value.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on the worked examples: find contextual domain/range and evaluate functions at specific inputs; teacher checks substitution and contextual reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_evaluation: contextual domain/range and function notation evaluation.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one contextual domain/range problem and one function evaluation problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with contextual domain/range and function notation.

periods.notes: Mastery cycle on 8a-8c.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.objective: Evaluate composite expressions and interpret function values in context.
periods.workedExamples: 8-3, Examples 3-4 — Evaluate Composite Expressions f(a + b); Interpret f(5) = 12 in Context
periods.notes: Composite expression evaluation and contextual interpretation of function values.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8d with one retrieval prompt on evaluating f(a + b) for a given function.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-3, Examples 3-4 to develop: evaluate composite expressions like f(a + b) by substituting the entire expression and expanding, and interpret f(5) = 12 in a real-world distance-time context.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-3, Examples 3-4: evaluate composite expressions and interpret function values in context; teacher checks substitution and contextual reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_evaluation: composite evaluation and contextual interpretation problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one composite expression evaluation and one contextual interpretation problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with composite function evaluation.

periods.workedExamples: 8-3, Example 5 — Function Notation with Piecewise Definitions
periods.notes: Piecewise function evaluation: selecting the correct piece for each input. Split from Period 6 to separate composite expression evaluation from the distinct piecewise concept.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8d with one retrieval prompt on how to determine which rule to use for a piecewise function at a given input.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-3, Example 5 to develop: work with piecewise function definitions by selecting the correct piece for each input based on the domain condition, and evaluate piecewise functions at boundary points.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-3, Example 5: evaluate piecewise functions at various inputs; teacher checks correct piece selection.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_evaluation: piecewise function evaluation problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one piecewise function evaluation problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with piecewise function evaluation.

periods.objective: Distinguish linear from nonlinear functions and identify linearity from representations.
periods.workedExamples: 8-4, Examples 1-2 — Identify Linear Function from Table; Identify Nonlinear Function from Graph
periods.notes: Identify linear functions by constant rate of change in tables and nonlinear functions from curved graphs.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8e with one retrieval prompt on computing rate of change (slope) between two points.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-4, Examples 1-2 to develop: determine if a table represents a linear function by checking whether the rate of change is constant between all pairs of consecutive points, and identify a nonlinear function from a parabolic graph by checking varying rates of change.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-4, Examples 1-2: compute rates of change from tables and determine linearity; teacher checks rate-of-change computation and constancy verification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on rate_of_change: linearity identification from tables and graphs.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one linear-vs-nonlinear identification from a table and one from a graph.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with identifying linear and nonlinear functions.

periods.objective: Calculate rate of change; compare linear and exponential growth; select function models.
periods.workedExamples: 8-4, Examples 3-5 — Rate of Change Calculation; Compare Linear and Exponential Growth; Real-World Function Model Selection
periods.notes: Rate of change interpretation, linear vs. exponential comparison, and data modeling.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8f with one retrieval prompt on the difference between a constant rate of change (linear) and a constant ratio (exponential).
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-4, Examples 3-5 to develop: calculate and interpret rate of change from a temperature table over time, compare linear growth (constant additive increase) with exponential growth (constant multiplicative ratio) using two bank account scenarios, and select the best function model for a bacterial population data set.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-4, Examples 3-5: compute rates of change and distinguish growth types; teacher checks rate vs. ratio reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on rate_of_change: rate calculations, growth comparison, and model selection problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one rate-of-change calculation and one linear-vs-exponential comparison.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with rate of change and function modeling.

periods.objective: Graph vertical shifts and horizontal shifts of parent functions.
periods.workedExamples: 8-5, Examples 1-2 — Graph f(x) + 3 (Vertical Shift Up); Graph f(x − 2) (Horizontal Shift Right)
periods.notes: Core shift transformations: vertical and horizontal translations applied to parent functions.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8g with one retrieval prompt on what vertical and horizontal shifts do to a graph.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-5, Examples 1-2 to develop: graph a vertical shift up by adding a constant to f(x), and graph a horizontal shift right by replacing x with x − h, emphasizing the sign convention for horizontal shifts.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-5, Examples 1-2: apply vertical and horizontal shifts to parent functions and sketch the result; teacher checks shift direction.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_transformations: graphing vertical and horizontal shifts.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one vertical shift graphing problem and one horizontal shift graphing problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with graphing shifts.

periods.objective: Graph reflections and vertical stretches of parent functions.
periods.workedExamples: 8-5, Examples 3-4 — Graph −f(x) (Reflection over x-axis); Graph 2f(x) (Vertical Stretch)
periods.notes: Reflection and stretch transformations applied to parent functions. Split from Period 10 to separate shift concepts from reflection and stretch concepts.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8g with one retrieval prompt on what reflection over the x-axis does to the graph of a parent function.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-5, Examples 3-4 to develop: graph a reflection over the x-axis by negating the function (flipping y-values), and graph a vertical stretch by multiplying by a factor greater than 1 (making the graph taller/narrower).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-5, Examples 3-4: apply reflections and stretches to parent functions and sketch the result; teacher checks transformation type and magnitude.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_transformations: graphing reflections and vertical stretches.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one reflection graphing problem and one vertical stretch graphing problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with reflections and stretches.

periods.objective: Describe transformations from equations and apply transformations to real-world contexts.
periods.workedExamples: 8-5, Examples 5-6 — Describe Transformation from Parent Function; Real-World Profit Function
periods.notes: Multi-step transformation description from equations and real-world profit-function application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8h with one retrieval prompt on describing the transformations in f(x) = −2(x − 1)² + 3.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-5, Examples 5-6 to develop: describe multiple transformations applied to a parent function from a complex equation (shift, reflect, stretch), and apply a vertical shift to a real-world profit function to model the effect of a marketing campaign.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-5, Examples 5-6: decompose transformation equations and apply shifts to contextual functions; teacher checks transformation ordering.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on function_transformations: multi-step transformation description and real-world application.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one transformation description from an equation and one real-world shift application.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with describing transformations.

periods.objective: Compare rates of change and y-intercepts from tables and graphs.
periods.workedExamples: 8-6, Examples 1-2 — Compare Rate of Change from Two Tables; Compare y-Intercepts from Two Graphs
periods.notes: Function comparison from tables and graphs: rate of change and y-intercepts.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8i with one retrieval prompt on how to compute rate of change from a table and compare two functions.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-6, Examples 1-2 to develop: compute and compare rates of change from two function tables, and compare y-intercepts by reading where each graph crosses the y-axis.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-6, Examples 1-2: compare rates and y-intercepts from tables and graphs; teacher checks comparison reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on comparing_functions: table-based and graph-based function comparison.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one rate-of-change comparison from tables and one y-intercept comparison from graphs.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with comparing functions from representations.

periods.objective: Compare maximum values; convert between representations; apply function comparison to real-world decisions.
periods.workedExamples: 8-6, Examples 3-5 — Which Function Has Greater Maximum; Convert Between Representations; Real-World Phone Plan Comparison
periods.notes: Graph-based comparison (maximum values), representation conversion, and real-world phone plan comparison. Split from Period 13 to separate table-based comparison from graph-based and real-world comparison.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 8i with one retrieval prompt on how to find the maximum value of a downward-opening parabola from its graph.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 8-6, Examples 3-5 to develop: determine which of two parabolas has a greater maximum value from their graphs, convert a function from graph form to equation form by reading two points, and compare two phone plans described in different representations (verbal and table) by converting both to equations.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 8-6, Examples 3-5: compare maxima, convert representations, and solve real-world comparison problems; teacher checks conversion accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on comparing_functions: maximum comparison, representation conversion, and real-world problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one maximum comparison and one representation conversion problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with comparing functions and converting representations.

periods.notes: Mathematical practices focus: transformation reasoning, function comparison across representations, and error analysis.

nonInstructionArtifact.groupTask: Mathematical practices focus: transformation reasoning, function comparison across representations, and error analysis.
nonInstructionArtifact.sourceWorkedExamples: Use the prior instruction periods named in the notes as the problem bank.
nonInstructionArtifact.deliverable: Group solution poster or shared explanation with one strategy comparison.
nonInstructionArtifact.facilitationNotes: Assign roles for transformation identification, comparison setup, graphing, and explanation.
nonInstructionArtifact.completionCriteria: Correct method, accurate result, clear justification, and response to peer question.

periods.notes: Mid-unit review across 8a-8f: functions, domain/range, evaluation, and linearity.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems combining function identification, domain/range, and linearity.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.notes: Mid-unit assessment on 8a-8f.
Mastery cycle on 8g-8j.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.notes: Unit 8 review across 8a-8j.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.notes: Unit 8 final review and targeted remediation across 8a-8j.

nonInstructionArtifact.objectiveScope: Unit 8 final review across 8a-8j with targeted remediation.
nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by mid-unit and end-of-unit evidence; target weakest objective clusters.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems combining transformations, comparisons, and real-world modeling.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes final review problem set and submits reflection on growth areas.

periods.notes: Unit 8 assessment.
